text: In the third case, the surgeon identifies microcalcifications in the ureteral wall just below the ureteropoietic junction. Indocyanine green (ICG) is injected through nephrostomy, but it does not provide precise feedback on the stricture level. The ureter is then cut and spatulated, preparing for the reconstructive phase.

The surgeon prepares a buccal mucosal graft by contouring a 3x1 cm section of oral mucosa and cutting it with forceps. The graft is placed into the abdominal cavity after the posterior plate of the ureteral anastomosis is completed with a terminal-terminal suture. The graft is applied over the ureteral defect and sutured to the peripheral margins, modeling the graft tissue to repair the defect and restore the ureteral caliber for instrument progression.

The surgeon wraps the previously prepared omental flap around the repaired ureter to ensure vascularization of the graft, aiming to prevent stricture recurrence. The surgical procedure concludes successfully without significant complications.
